mnemonics: MA: Memory Averages. A Moving Average (MA) process has a 'memory' of past shocks (error terms), but it only remembers them for a finite time, defined by the order 'q'.
keyPoints: An MA(q) process is a linear combination of the current and past white noise error terms.
The process is always stationary, regardless of the parameter values, because it depends on a finite number of finite-variance terms.
The autocorrelation function (ACF) for an MA(q) process cuts off abruptly after lag q, which is a key identifying characteristic.
commonMistakes: Confusing MA (Moving Average) with SMA (Simple Moving Average) forecasting. An MA process is a data-generating model, not a smoothing technique.
Assuming an MA process is non-stationary. Unlike AR processes, MA processes are stationary by construction.
Misidentifying the order 'q' by expecting the PACF to cut off sharply. It is the ACF that cuts off for an MA process, while the PACF tails off.
